Why are you interested in this role?: What pulls me to this role is the diagnosis. The part of strategy I love most is the moment before anyone has an answer—walking into an ambiguous problem, defining what's actually going on, and pressure-testing it against data. My consulting work is ambiguous by design: I helped audit 300+ cases to quantify $362K and overturn assumptions everyone had taken as fact, and for an NGO client I ran focus groups and analyzed the qualitative data to find the real decision-making breakdown. A Strategy & Business Operations role is that work made into a job, on a bigger canvas.
Why do you want to work at Duolingo?: Honestly, the mission and the product. My degrees are in Education Studies and Applied Organizational Psychology, so a company making education universally available is something I genuinely care about—and Duolingo is a product I admire as a user. The role also fits how I work: I treat business problems as systems to study, diagnose with data before designing, and move in rapid cycles. I manage operations across 20+ programs today, partnering with Marketing, Tech, and Sales leaders to align on priorities and trade-offs, so cross-functional, fast-moving work is where I'm most comfortable.
How do you approach ambiguity?: I thrive in it. My approach is to diagnose before designing: ask questions before assuming, define success in measurable terms, and build in feedback loops so course-correction is cheap. When I led a self-started initiative at NextGen, no one had asked me to study engagement—I framed the question myself, analyzed 2,000+ data points, found that manager effectiveness was the top driver, and turned it into a report that reached the C-suite and kept getting referenced after I left. That's how I handle ambiguity: turn an open question into a framework and a decision, backed by data.
Tell us about yourself / anything else: I'm an analytical organizer with a growth mindset, grounded in an MS in Applied Organizational Psychology from USC. In practice that means I diagnose with data, collaborate cross-functionally on the solution, and track the metrics that prove it worked. I've worked across the US, Scandinavia, and Taiwan—including an office of 22 nationalities—so global, cross-functional collaboration is familiar. I'll be candid that my background is OD-consulting and program management rather than classic strategy consulting, but the analytical muscle and comfort with ambiguity transfer directly, and I'd ramp fast.
How has AI changed the way you approach: AI has become part of how I work day-to-day, not a side tool. I use it to move faster from messy input to a clear decision—synthesizing stakeholder feedback, drafting and pressure-testing communications, and spotting patterns in data I'd otherwise comb through by hand. What's changed most is my starting point: I now ask what a well-designed AI workflow could take off my plate before I default to doing it manually. I've even been building a RAG-style system for my own job search with a friend, where the AI has access to my background and preferences so it can evaluate fit for me specifically—which made me realize how much the same personalization principle scales to real work. I still treat the output as a draft to verify, not gospel; the judgment stays mine.
